pattern_recognition: Array Manipulation - This problem falls under array manipulation as it requires rearranging elements in an array. Signals include the need for in-place operations and handling of rotations which is a common pattern in array-related problems.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Simply rotate the array k times by moving the last element to the front each time.
approaches.algorithm: For each of the k rotations, remove the last element and insert it at the front of the array.
approaches.code_python:
from typing import List

def rotate(nums: List[int], k: int) -> None:
    n = len(nums)
    k = k % n  # Handle cases where k is greater than n
    for _ in range(k):
        last_element = nums.pop()  # Remove the last element
        nums.insert(0, last_element)  # Insert it at the front

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use array reversal to achieve the rotation in a more efficient manner without extra space.
approaches.algorithm: Reverse the entire array, then reverse the first k elements and finally reverse the remaining n-k elements.
approaches.code_python:
from typing import List

def rotate(nums: List[int], k: int) -> None:
    n = len(nums)
    k = k % n
    
    def reverse(start: int, end: int) -> None:
        while start < end:
            nums[start], nums[end] = nums[end], nums[start]
            start += 1
            end -= 1
    
    reverse(0, n - 1)
    reverse(0, k - 1)
    reverse(k, n - 1)

dry_run: Consider the input array [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] and k = 2. For the brute force method, we would move the last element (5) to the front, resulting in [5, 1, 2, 3, 4]. Then move 4 to the front, resulting in [4, 5, 1, 2, 3]. For the optimal approach, we first reverse the entire array to get [5, 4, 3, 2, 1], then reverse the first 2 elements to get [4, 5, 3, 2, 1], and finally reverse the last 3 elements to get [4, 5, 1, 2, 3].
edge_cases: An empty input array should return an empty array. A single element array should remain unchanged regardless of k. Duplicates in the array should not affect the rotation. Large input sizes should be handled efficiently without exceeding memory limits. Overflow issues may arise with very large k values.
common_pitfalls: Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling cases where k is larger than the array length, leading to unnecessary rotations. 2) Failing to perform in-place operations, resulting in excessive space usage. 3) Incorrectly reversing the segments of the array, which can lead to an incorrect final arrangement.
